channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran’s Fractured Leadership Is Struggling to Coordinate, Officials Say
item.description: As leaders are killed and replaced, Iranian negotiators may not know what their government is willing to concede in any negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Adam Goldman and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Many senior Iranian leaders and their deputies have been killed, and those who survive have had difficulty communicating.

item.title: A New U.S. Missile Hit a Sports Hall in Iran. Here’s What We Know About It.
item.description: Barely out of prototype testing, the Precision Strike Missile is shrouded in secrecy — including which Persian Gulf countries the Army is launching them from.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Offensive vs. Defensive: On Iran, the Distinction Matters for Britain’s Leader
item.description: As the United States expands its armada of warplanes on British soil, Prime Minister Keir Starmer says he is trying to defend Britain’s interests while keeping the country out of war.
item.dc:creator: Adam Goldman, Eric Schmitt and Michael D. Shear
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain at a meeting in London on Monday. “We will not be drawn into the wider war,” he told lawmakers in mid-March.

item.title: TSA Workers Begin to Receive Paychecks After Trump Signs Executive Order
item.description: A memo that President Trump signed on Friday ordering the Department of Homeland Security to pay T.S.A. officers did not specify whether they would be paid on a regular schedule.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:description: Transportation Security Administration officers at a security checkpoint in Pittsburgh International Airport on Monday.

item.title: Senate Republicans Make No Move to End DHS Shutdown in Brief Session
item.description: With Congress in recess, much of the Homeland Security Department remains without money because of the impasse on Capitol Hill, even as airport security workers are to begin getting paid.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:description: “If we had something good to go today, we could have done it today,” Senator John Hoeven said on Monday.

item.title: Senators Seek Answers From Trump Administration About Airport Immigration Arrests
item.description: Airport security officials have been sharing passenger data with immigration agents, but the program received little attention until videos captured a woman and her daughter being detained at San Francisco’s airport.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: The Transportation Security Administration began sharing passenger information with immigration officials a year ago, highlighting fliers who were on a list of people to be deported.

item.title: Mexico Pressures U.S. Over Deaths of Its Citizens in ICE Custody
item.description: Claudia Sheinbaum, president of Mexico, said her country would take legal steps to demand better conditions at immigration detention facilities, where she said 14 Mexican citizens had died since President Trump took office.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango, Jazmine Ulloa and Allison McCann
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico at a news conference on Monday.

item.title: After Months of Threats, Trump Softens His Stance on Blocking Oil to Cuba
item.description: The White House press secretary said the United States would evaluate oil shipments to Cuba on a “case-by-case basis,” after a Russian tanker full of crude reached the island.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Anton Troianovski and Maria Abi-Habib
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: An oil tanker near the coastline of Matanzas, Cuba, on Monday. The ban on foreign oil imports has plunged the country into a crisis.

item.title: DeSantis Signs Bill to Rename Florida Airport for Trump
item.description: The airport near West Palm Beach will be rebranded the President Donald J. Trump International Airport on July 1, if approved by the Federal Aviation Administration.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at Palm Beach International Airport in West Palm Beach, Fla., last month.

item.title: Trump Reveals New Look for White House Ballroom After Times Article
item.description: Architects and preservation organizations have warned that little scrutiny has been given to the project’s design plans.
item.dc:creator: Emily Badger
item.media:description: President Trump showing a new rendering of the proposed White House ballroom’s south portico, without the grand staircase that appeared in previous plans.

item.title: ‘I Think That MAGA Is Dying:’ Inside the Youth Movement at CPAC
item.description: At a sparsely attended Conservative Political Action Conference, young Republicans were eager to start the post-Trump era.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The annual Conservative Political Action Conference has been gradually waning in energy for years, but empty convention halls and an anemic speakers list gave many young people in attendance a doomed feeling.

item.title: A Crash Course on Primary Elections in Maine, Michigan and Beyond
item.description: Here’s a crash course on what’s coming up next.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Robert F. Bukaty/Associated Press; Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Democratic primary race for Senate in Maine is between Graham Platner and Gov. Janet Mills

item.title: Mark Sanford, Years After Scandal, Aims for a Comeback to Congress
item.description: The former South Carolina governor and congressman filed papers to run for his old seat, six years after running for president and nearly two decades after a high-profile affair.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Joshua Boucher/The State — Tribune News Service, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mark Sanford in 2023. His star has risen and fallen in Republican politics over the years.

item.title: When Will Japan’s Cherry Blossoms Bloom? A.I. Can Help Answer That
item.description: Experts use artificial intelligence to analyze data, plus thousands of crowdsourced photos, to forecast the prized flowers, which are a multibillion-dollar attraction.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández, Kiuko Notoya, Hisako Ueno and Kentaro Takahashi
item.media:description: Visitors gathered beneath cherry blossoms at Ueno Park in Tokyo last week.

item.title: NASA Is Launching Astronauts to the Moon, but Americans Aren’t That Excited
item.description: Polling has consistently found that most people would prefer NASA spend money on things like monitoring climate change and averting asteroid collisions rather than human spaceflight.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: Artemis II pilot Victor Glover met with young space enthusiasts at the Kennedy Space Center in January. While overall opinions of NASA remain high, polls find human exploration of the moon ranks pretty low.

item.title: States Plow Ahead With A.I. Regulation, Defying Trump
item.description: States ranging from California to Utah are taking steps to place guardrails on the technology even after the president ordered them to stop.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Max Whittaker for The New York Times

item.title: What to Know About California’s Executive Order on A.I.
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom, a Democrat, issued an order requiring safety and privacy guardrails for artificial intelligence companies contracting with the state.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom. California has been a leader in tech lawmaking, and was the first state to pass a law mandating safety and transparency from the biggest A.I. companies.

item.title: Woman Spent Five Months in Jail After A.I. Linked Her to Bank Fraud Case
item.description: The police chief in Fargo, N.D., acknowledged “missteps” but stopped short of apologizing to Angela Lipps, a Tennessee resident who said she had never been to North Dakota before she was arrested.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: Angela Lipps outside her home in Elizabethton, Tenn., before she was arrested in July 2025. She spent five months in jail in Tennessee and North Dakota before she was released on Dec. 24.

item.title: Renoir, Cézanne and Matisse Paintings Are Stolen in 3-Minute Museum Heist, Police Say
item.description: Thieves broke into the Magnani-Rocca Foundation outside Parma, Italy, officials said, and made off with paintings worth millions.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins and Josephine de La Bruyère
item.media:description: “Les Poissons” by Pierre-Auguste Renoir was one of three paintings stolen this month from a museum in Italy.

item.title: Can You Fake Being Rich?
item.description: A spate of popular social media accounts offer advice on how to achieve an “old-money” look.
item.dc:creator: Guy Trebay
item.media:description: The appearance of wealth, and how to get it, has become a hot topic on social media.

item.title: She Was a Famous Millennial Feminist. Her Polyamory Memoir Is Heartbreaking.
item.description: Almost every ideology can be wielded to make women feel that they’re failing.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg

item.title: Trump, Don’t Make Churchill’s Deadly Mistake
item.description: A 1936 convention establishing Turkish control over the Dardanelles could be an inspiration to resolving the energy bottleneck in the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Asli Aydintasbas
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Stephen Colbert Marvels at ‘No Kings’ Rallies Worldwide
item.description: “Just look at the crowd we got here in New York,” he said. “Oop, that’s the T.S.A. line at J.F.K.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: Palm Sunday Attack in Nigeria Leaves at Least 12 Dead
item.description: No one has claimed responsibility for the killings in the mostly Christian city of Jos, and the police have yet to arrest the gunmen.
item.dc:creator: Saikou Jammeh
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: A Nigerian military vehicle at the scene of an attack the morning after gunmen killed several people in Jos, Nigeria, on Monday.

item.title: Michigan Synagogue Attack Was ‘Inspired by Hezbollah,’ Officials Say
item.description: The man who rammed his vehicle into the synagogue was purposely targeting the Jewish community, officials said on Monday, detailing his days of planning.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Temple Israel in West Bloomfield Township, Mich.

item.title: Why Barbie Dream Fest in Florida Is Drawing Comparisons to Fyre Festival
item.description: Fans had high hopes for the event, a Mattel-sanctioned gathering in a Florida convention center. Now, it’s drawing comparisons to Fyre Festival.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:description: The bright pink world of Barbie struggled to fill its space in the cavernous Broward County Convention Center. “It felt like being just trapped in a big, dark warehouse,” one attendee said.
